insights: Breadth: 14/16 stocks up — broadly bullish today
context: Broad buying today — most large-caps rose. Broad-based rallies are generally healthier than a narrow one led by 1–2 heavyweights.
Money rotated into IT and out of Pharma. Sector leadership shifts often — chasing last month's hottest sector is a classic beginner trap.
India VIX is low (13.15) — calm, low expected volatility. Calm markets breed complacency; a good time to check your asset allocation, not to chase risk.

gk.teaser: Ancient games ran ~776 BCE to 393 CE; revived 1896.
gk.more: The ancient Greek games honoured Zeus at Olympia until a Roman emperor banned pagan festivals. Frenchman Pierre de Coubertin revived them in Athens in 1896. India's first individual gold came only in 2008 — Abhinav Bindra, shooting.

gk.t: Why DNA is called a blueprint
gk.teaser: Four letters — A, T, G, C — spell out every living thing.
gk.more: DNA is a molecule storing instructions to build proteins; genes are its meaningful sentences. Humans share ~99.9% of DNA with each other and ~60% with bananas. The 2003 Human Genome Project read all 3 billion letters; today a genome can be sequenced in hours.

gk.teaser: Money earning money on the money it already earned.
gk.more: ₹1 lakh at 12% becomes ₹3.1 lakh in 10 years and ₹9.6 lakh in 20 — the growth accelerates because each year's interest joins the principal. The 'rule of 72': divide 72 by the interest rate to estimate doubling time. At 12%, money doubles every ~6 years.

gk.t: India defined zero as a number
gk.teaser: Not just a placeholder for nothing — a number you can calculate with.
gk.more: Many cultures used a symbol for 'nothing,' but the 7th-century mathematician Brahmagupta was the first to treat zero as a number in its own right, writing rules for adding, subtracting and multiplying with it. Combined with the Indian decimal place-value system, this idea travelled through the Arab world to Europe and became the foundation of modern arithmetic. It is why we can write 1,000,000 without inventing a new symbol for every power of ten.

gk.t: India has the world's longest written constitution
gk.teaser: It began with 395 articles — and keeps growing.
gk.more: When it came into force on 26 January 1950, the Constitution of India had 395 articles, 22 parts and 8 schedules, making it the lengthiest national constitution ever written. Its framers borrowed selectively from abroad — the parliamentary system from Britain, fundamental rights from the US, directive principles from Ireland. Decades of amendments have since pushed it well past 470 articles and 12 schedules.

gk.t: Drafting India's constitution took nearly three years
gk.teaser: 2 years, 11 months and 18 days of debate.
gk.more: The Constituent Assembly, chaired by Rajendra Prasad with B. R. Ambedkar heading the drafting committee, worked from December 1946 to November 1949 — 2 years, 11 months and 18 days. The final document was not printed but written out by hand in flowing calligraphy by Prem Behari Narain Raizada, and illustrated by artists from Shantiniketan. Members debated it almost line by line.

gk.t: Sikkim was an independent kingdom until 1975
gk.teaser: India's 22nd state was once ruled by a monarch.
gk.more: For centuries Sikkim was ruled by the Namgyal dynasty of Chogyals. After a 1975 referendum abolished the monarchy, it merged with India to become the 22nd state. Today it is India's least populous state and, in 2016, became the country's first fully organic-farming state.

gk.t: The shortest war in history lasted under 40 minutes
gk.teaser: Britain versus Zanzibar, 1896 — over before lunch.
gk.more: On 27 August 1896, Britain bombarded the Sultan's palace in Zanzibar after a disputed succession. Resistance collapsed in roughly 38 to 45 minutes, making it the shortest recorded war in history. Around 500 of the Sultan's defenders were killed or wounded; the only British casualty was a single injured sailor who later recovered.

gk.t: Cleopatra lived closer to the Moon landing than to the pyramids
gk.teaser: The Great Pyramid was already ancient in her lifetime.
gk.more: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built around 2560 BCE. Cleopatra died in 30 BCE — over 2,500 years later. The 1969 Moon landing was under 2,000 years after her death, so she lived chronologically nearer to Apollo 11 than to the pyramid-builders. Ancient Egypt spanned so much time that 'ancient' meant something even to the ancients.

gk.t: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire
gk.teaser: Lectures were running before Tenochtitlan existed.
gk.more: Teaching at Oxford dates back to at least 1096. The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was founded around 1325, more than two centuries later. So when Aztec priests were laying the first stones of their island city, English students had already been studying at Oxford for generations.

gkMore.t: Mount Everest grows ~4 mm every year
gkMore.teaser: At 8,849 m it's the world's highest point — and still rising.
gkMore.more: The India-Eurasia plate collision keeps pushing it up. Its official height was revised in 2020 jointly by Nepal and China. The first confirmed summit: Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay, 29 May 1953.

gkMore.t: India's independence and republic days differ by purpose
gkMore.teaser: 15 Aug 1947 = freedom; 26 Jan 1950 = our own Constitution.
gkMore.more: Independence Day marks the end of British rule. Republic Day marks the Constitution coming into force, making India a republic with its own head of state instead of the British monarch. That's why Republic Day has the grand parade — it celebrates the state India built for itself.

gkMore.t: A cheque and a demand draft are not the same
gkMore.teaser: A cheque can bounce; a DD is prepaid so it cannot.
gkMore.more: A cheque instructs YOUR bank to pay and fails if your balance is short. A demand draft is bought from the bank upfront — the bank itself guarantees payment. That's why institutions ask for DDs for fees. Both are fading as UPI handles 80%+ of India's retail digital payments.

gkMore.t: The Olympics happen every 4 years — mostly
gkMore.teaser: Summer and Winter Games alternate every 2 years.
gkMore.more: Cancelled only by the World Wars (1916, 1940, 1944) and delayed once by COVID (Tokyo 2020 held in 2021). India's first Olympic medal as an independent nation: hockey gold, 1948 London — beating Britain in Britain a year after independence.

gkMore.t: Why a year has 365 days and a leap year 366
gkMore.teaser: Earth's orbit takes 365.25 days — the calendar catches up every 4 years.
gkMore.more: The extra quarter-day is banked and added as Feb 29. Century years skip it unless divisible by 400 (2000 yes, 2100 no) because the true figure is 365.2422, not exactly 365.25. Without this, seasons would drift through the calendar.

gkMore.t: The seven wonders include the Taj Mahal
gkMore.teaser: Chosen by a global vote of 100 million people in 2007.
gkMore.more: The 'New 7 Wonders': Taj Mahal, Great Wall of China, Petra (Jordan), Christ the Redeemer (Brazil), Machu Picchu (Peru), Chichén Itzá (Mexico), and the Colosseum (Rome). Of the original ancient wonders, only the Great Pyramid of Giza still stands.

gkMore.teaser: Simple pays on the principal; compound pays on interest too.
gkMore.more: ₹1 lakh at 10% simple = ₹10,000 every year, flat. Compound = ₹10,000 the first year, ₹11,000 the next, and so on — because interest joins the principal. Fixed deposits compound quarterly, which is why their 'effective yield' is a bit higher than the headline rate.

gkMore.teaser: Listed in the Constitution's Eighth Schedule — Hindi isn't the 'national' language.
gkMore.more: Hindi is an official language of the Union alongside English; no language is legally 'national'. The 22 include Bengali, Telugu, Marathi, Tamil, Urdu, Gujarati and Sanskrit. India has 120+ languages with 10,000+ speakers each — the world's most multilingual large country.

gkMore.t: Why the sea is salty but rivers are not
gkMore.teaser: Rivers carry tiny amounts of salt to the sea — and the sea never drains.
gkMore.more: Rainwater dissolves minute quantities of minerals from rocks into rivers. Rivers flow to the sea, where water evaporates but salt stays behind, concentrating over billions of years. Rivers taste fresh only because their salt keeps moving on instead of accumulating.

gkMore.t: ISRO launched 104 satellites in one rocket
gkMore.teaser: A world record in 2017, from a single PSLV flight.
gkMore.more: Most were tiny foreign 'nanosatellites' — proof of ISRO's reputation as the world's budget-friendly, reliable launcher. India was also first to reach Mars orbit on its first attempt (Mangalyaan, 2014) — at a cost less than the movie Gravity.

learn.title: P/E ratio: the market's price tag
learn.teaser: How many years of profit you're paying for one share.
body: Price-to-Earnings = share price ÷ earnings per share. A P/E of 25 means you pay ₹25 for every ₹1 of annual profit. High P/E signals the market expects fast growth (or the stock is expensive); low P/E signals slow growth expectations (or a bargain).

It's only meaningful against context: the company's own history, its sector average, and growth rate. Indian IT often trades at 20–30; PSU banks far lower. A 'cheap' stock with shrinking profits is usually cheap for a reason.

learn.title: Correlation is not causation
learn.teaser: The most important sentence in analytics.
body: Ice cream sales and drownings rise together — summer causes both. When two metrics move together, there are always four possibilities: A causes B, B causes A, something else causes both, or coincidence. Dashboards show correlation; only controlled experiments or careful causal analysis show causation.
Practical BI reflex: before reporting 'X drove Y', ask what else changed in the same window — a campaign, a price change, seasonality, a tracking change. The last one bites analysts most often.

learn.title: Mean vs median: which lies less?
learn.teaser: One billionaire in a room makes the 'average' person rich.
body: The mean (sum ÷ count) is dragged by outliers; the median (middle value) isn't. Average income sounds impressive when a few earn crores; median income tells you what the typical person makes. Same for ticket sizes, session times, order values.

Rule of thumb: for skewed data (money, wait times, engagement), report the median or percentiles. If mean and median differ a lot, that gap itself is a finding — your distribution has a long tail worth investigating.

learn.title: Opportunity cost
learn.teaser: The real cost of anything is what you gave up to get it.
body: Spending ₹15 lakh on a car isn't just ₹15 lakh — it's also the ~₹47 lakh that money could become in 10 years at 12%. Every choice of time or money silently declines the alternatives. Economists call the best foregone alternative the opportunity cost.

It reframes decisions: a 'free' three-hour meeting costs the work you'd have shipped. An okay job offer costs the better one you stopped searching for. Comparing against 'doing nothing' is almost always the wrong baseline.

learn.title: How IPOs actually work
learn.teaser: A private company sells shares to the public for the first time.
body: The company hires investment banks to set a price band, files a prospectus (DRHP) with SEBI, and collects bids. Retail investors apply in lots; oversubscribed issues allot by lottery. On listing day the stock starts trading — sometimes far above or below issue price.

Listing 'pops' make headlines, but the base rate is sobering: many hyped IPOs trade below issue price within a year (India's 2021 tech-IPO batch is the classic case). Reading where the money goes — fresh growth capital vs early investors cashing out — tells you a lot.

learn.title: A/B testing in five sentences
learn.teaser: Split users randomly, change one thing, measure the difference.
body: Random assignment makes the two groups statistically identical except for your change, so any outcome gap is caused by it — this is the cleanest causation tool in product work. The catch is sample size: small tests 'win' by luck constantly.

Standard practice: decide the metric and minimum effect before launching, run a significance check (p < 0.05 convention), and beware peeking early — checking daily and stopping at the first 'significant' result inflates false positives dramatically.

learn.title: Why the Sensex is called the Sensex
learn.teaser: 'Sensitive Index' — 30 big BSE stocks standing in for the market.
body: It tracks 30 of the largest, most-traded companies on the Bombay Stock Exchange, Asia's oldest (1875). Base value: 100 in 1978–79 — so ~77,000 means those stocks are worth roughly 770× their 1979 level. The Nifty 50 is the NSE's 50-stock equivalent.
Index composition changes over time — laggards get dropped, risers added — which builds in survivorship: the index isn't the same 30 companies your grandfather's Sensex had.

learn.title: What the repo rate actually does
learn.teaser: RBI's lending rate to banks — the master dial for every EMI.
body: Banks borrow from RBI at the repo rate. Raise it and all credit gets costlier, cooling spending and inflation; cut it and money cheapens, boosting growth. The Monetary Policy Committee resets it every two months against a 4% (±2%) CPI target.

Transmission is the catch: banks pass rate cuts to depositors fast and to borrowers slowly. That lag — and how much of a cut actually reaches your home loan — is what analysts fight about after every MPC meeting.
